---
title: "RStudio Orientation"
subtitle: "Make a Project, get to know the panes, and see what your data looks like once it's loaded."
---

Before installing the package, it's worth a few minutes getting comfortable with
RStudio itself — where things are, and how your data looks once it's loaded. If
you already work in RStudio day to day, you can skim this and move on to
[Install jstats](install-jstats.qmd).

## Start a Project

Before installing the package or loading any data, create an RStudio **Project**.

Without a Project, R needs to be told where your files live (its "working
directory"), and that quickly drags in folder paths, the difference between `\`
and `/`, and the fact that Windows and Mac write paths differently. A Project
sidesteps all of it: it ties a folder, your data, and your work together, so R
always knows where it is.

To create one, in RStudio choose **File → New Project → New Directory → New
Project**, give it a name (for example `jstats-practice`), pick where it should
live, and click **Create Project**. RStudio opens your new Project.

From now on, you reopen this Project by double-clicking its `.Rproj` file (the
small file RStudio created inside your Project folder). That one habit — always
work inside a Project — saves more beginner headaches than any other.

## The RStudio panes

With your Project open, you'll see three panes to begin with. A fourth — the
**source pane**, in the top-left — appears the first time you open a script or
view a dataset. Here's what each is for:

- **Editor / source** (top-left) — where you write and save your code, and where a
  dataset opens as a grid when you click it to view. This is your reusable list of
  commands.
- **Console** (bottom-left) — where code runs and results print.
- **Environment** (top-right) — lists the data and objects currently loaded, by
  **name and size** (not the data itself). Importantly, what lives here is a
  *working copy* of your data, held in memory. Mistakes hit the copy, never the
  original file on disk.
- **Files / Plots / Help** (bottom-right) — your folder contents, any charts you
  make, and help pages.

Let's make the panes concrete with a dataset that already comes with R. R ships
with several built-in datasets for practice and examples; one is `mtcars` (data on
some classic car models). You can copy it into a name of your own choosing using
R's **assignment operator**, `<-`, which means "put what's on the right into the
name on the left":

```r
cars <- mtcars
```

You now have a dataset called `cars` — the same data, under a name you picked. Look
at the **Environment** pane (top-right): `cars` appears, described as *32 obs. of 11
variables*. That's its name and size — the Environment lists what you've loaded, not
the data themselves. To see the data, click `cars` in the **Environment**. A tab
opens in the **source pane** (top-left) showing a grid — rows of cars down the side,
variables across the top. This is the same top-left pane where your scripts will
open shortly; RStudio uses it for both.

<!-- screenshot: Environment showing `cars` as "32 obs. of 11 variables", viewer grid open in source pane -->

`iris` is another built-in dataset (measurements of flowers). Because it's built in,
it's already available by its own name — you don't have to copy it first. To take a
quick look, run:

```r
View(iris)
```

Note the capital **V** — `View` is one of a handful of base-R functions that breaks
the usual all-lowercase habit (a leftover from R's long, many-handed history rather
than any real reason), so `view(iris)` won't work; R distinguishes capitals from
lowercase. jstats keeps its own functions lowercase, in step with the base-R
majority.

The same kind of grid opens. Notice one column, `Species`, holds group names
(setosa, versicolor, virginica) instead of numbers — your first glimpse of a
**categorical variable**: a column that sorts cases into groups. You'll meet these
properly later.

<!-- screenshot: View(iris) grid open, Species column visible -->

You'll see `mtcars` and `iris` again and again in R's help pages and in other
packages' examples — now you know what they are, so they won't catch you off guard.
jstats brings its own example dataset too, `community`, which becomes available by
name once you install and load the package — we'll use it from the Quick Start
onward.

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Coming from SPSS or Stata?"}
A rough map if you've mostly used SPSS through its menus: the **Console** is like
the **output/viewer window** where results appear, and the **Plots** area is where
charts show up, much as they do in SPSS output. The closest thing to **Data View**
is the read-only grid that opens when you click a dataset in the Environment — but
note the **Environment** pane itself is *not* Data View: it only lists what's
loaded, by name and size. The **Editor** is the one piece without a close
menu-equivalent — it's your saved list of typed commands (nearest to the SPSS
syntax window, which menu users rarely open).
:::

You'll also notice **Terminal** and **Background Jobs** tabs near the Console.
These are for more advanced use — the Terminal is a direct line to your computer's
command prompt (PowerShell on Windows, the old DOS-style window) — and you can
ignore them for now.

One small thing that trips up newcomers: **R is case-sensitive.** `Income` and
`income` are two different names to R. When something doesn't work, a stray
capital is a common culprit.

## Try the console

Let's make the **Console** feel real, since it's where everything ultimately runs.
Click in the Console (bottom-left), type a sum, and press Enter:

```r
2 + 2
```

R answers straight back:

```
[1] 4
```

The console is a calculator that talks back. Now try a **function** — a named
command that takes some input in parentheses and hands you a result. This one
finds a square root:

```r
sqrt(144)
```

```
[1] 12
```

That's the shape of nearly everything you'll do in R: a function name, inputs in
parentheses, a result back. (And yes, `sqrt` is plain R — you're already learning
the real thing.)

So what's the **script** (the Editor) for, if the console can do everything? Just
this: **the console is for doing, the script is for keeping.** Anything you type
in the console runs and then scrolls away — perfect for quick, one-off jobs like a
fast calculation or installing a package. When you're doing analysis you'll want
to *keep* — and re-run, and share — you type those commands in the script and save
the file. We'll start doing exactly that once there's something worth saving.

---

With RStudio mapped out, the next page adds the jstats package itself.
